title: Optimize Object Refresh to Avoid Cloudflare 524 Timeout
description:
Long-running object refresh queries were exceeding Cloudflare's ~100s timeout, returning HTTP 524 errors. The fix involved optimizing the backend refresh logic rather than increasing timeouts.

### Performance Optimization Results
| Step | Before | After |
|------|--------|-------|
| Salesforce /describe | 1-2s | 1-2s |
| FieldDefinition pagination | 2-4s | 2-4s |
| DB field writes | 800 row-by-row updates → 8-20s | ≤200 updateOrCreate (only changed/new) → 1-4s |
| DB picklists | Per-value inserts (thousands) → 10-30s | Only diffs; create new/delete removed → 2-6s |
| DB audit logs | Per-row inserts → 3-8s | Only changed/new → 1-3s |
| DB deletions | Per-row updates → 1-3s | Single whereIn update → <0.5s |
| **Total** | **25-70s** (often >100s with big picklists) | **7-17s** |

### Architectural Recommendation
ChatGPT analysis suggested an async pattern (202 Accepted + polling/WebSockets) as the long-term best practice for heavy endpoints behind Cloudflare.
acceptance_criteria: Object refresh completes within Cloudflare's timeout window
No HTTP 524 errors during normal refresh operations
middleware_changes: Optimized DB writes to use updateOrCreate only for changed/new fields. Diff-based picklist updates. Batch deletion via whereIn. Reduced total refresh time from 25-70s to 7-17s.
implementation_notes: Implemented and deployed to production. Future consideration: async processing pattern with 202 Accepted + polling for very large objects.
comment_summary: Waqar optimized the code and provided a detailed before/after performance comparison table. Confirmed the solution was implemented on production.
